coredata.dc:title: Plasma-engineered GQD-inorganic membranes with tunable interactions for ultrahigh-efficiency molecular separations
coredata.dc:creator: Yeh, Yi-Jui
Liou, Jr Rong
Lin, Wei
Tung, Kuo-Lun
Chiang, Wei-Hung
coredata.dc:description: Ultrahigh-efficiency molecular separation (MS) membranes with tunable size-selective molecular separation, chemical-mechanical robustness, and long-term stabilities are useful for fundamental studies and applications. However, conventional MS membranes are hampered by the problems of uncontrolled molecular interactions, low separation efficiency, and energy and time-consuming fabrications. Here, we show an effective plasma engineering of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dot (NGQD)-inorganic nanocomposites composed with NGQDs and alumina-based hollow fibers (AHFs) with controlled properties and tunable local physicochemical environments for size-selective molecular separations. Detailed spectroscopic study and membrane characterization suggest that the plasma-synthesized NGQDs with defined zero-dimensional (0D) carbon-based sp2 hybridization, tunable nitrogen dopants and surface functional groups can affect the properties of the GQD-inorganic nanocomposites, therefore controlling molecular separation between the nanocomposites and molecules with different dye molecular weights (MWs). Moreover, the NGQDs can further induce the formation of nanochannel and control the nanopore structures of the nanocomposites, leading a significant molecular separation performance with a high 92.68 separation factor (S) for the mixed molecules with a 200 Da MW difference and a high water permeability up to 36.64 L m−2 h−1 bar−1. Our work provides a step for the engineering of functional nanocomposites for fundamental studies of molecular transport and emerging applications including catalysis, green energy, optoelectronics, biomedical and environmental applications.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optical spectroscopy study of the plasma-synthesized GQDs. (a) UV–vis spectra of synthesized GQDs by various plasma-treated time. Inset shows the photograph of different synthesized GQD samples. (b) PL spectra of synthesized GQDs by various plasma-treated time. b-1, b-2, b-3 and b-4 denote the GQD samples prepared with 5, 10, 15, and 20 min of plasma synthesis time.

SEM study of the GQD-inorganic membranes. SEM images of (a-1) pristine PA, and GQD membranes with plasma reaction times of (a-2) 5 min, (a-3) 10 min, (a-4) 15 min, and (a-5) 20 min. The cross-section SEM images of (b-1) pristine PA, and GQD membranes with plasma reaction time of (b-2) 5 min, (b-3) 10 min, (b-4) 15 min, and (b-5) 20 min (Scale bar a is 1 μm. Scale bar b is 100 nm).

XPS study of the GQD-inorganic membranes. (a) XPS wide scan spectra, and deconvoluted (b) C1s and (c) O1s spectra of the pristine PA and GQD-inorganic membranes prepared with different plasma synthesis times.

Molecule separation performance study of the GQD-inorganic membranes. (a) Pure water permeance and the rejection of CR molecules with pristine PA and GQD-inorganic membranes. (b) Various dyes separation test with pristine PA and GQD-inorganic membranes. (c) Rejection of probing PEG molecules with different MWs including 200, 400, 600, and 1000 Da. The dash lines indicate the values of MWCOs. (d) Pore size distribution of prepared membranes that estimated by the probability density function. Stability tests of the GQD-inorganic membranes with (e) acidic condition (pH = 1) and (f) alkaline condition (pH = 14). (g) Long-term stability evaluation of GQD-inorganic membranes for a period of 72 h.

Molecular separation for mixed dyes and separation performance comparison. (a) Schematic illustration of mixed dyes separation using MS membranes. (b) UV–vis spectra of the mixed dyes and permeate solutions. (c) Performance comparison of the plasma-fabricated GQD-inorganic membranes with other MS membranes.

Plasma-engineered GQD-inorganic porous membranes with tunable molecular interaction for high efficiency molecular separation (MS). (a) MS membranes are useful and desired for fundamental studies and applications. In this work, we develop a plasma engineering of molecular interaction-tunable GQD-inorganic MS membranes with outstanding performance including size-selective molecular separation, high water permeability, and long-term stabilities. (b) The plasma-synthesized GQDs equipped with unique 0D carbon-based sp2 hybridization nanostructures and tunable surface functional groups can not only enhance the water transport but also selectively interact with molecules with different MWs. (c) Moreover, the GQDs can further induce the formation of nanochannel and control the pore structures of the nanocomposite membranes, leading a significant molecular separation performance.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ultrahigh-efficiency molecular separation (MS) membranes with tunable size-selective molecular separation, chemical-mechanical robustness, and long-term stabilities are useful for fundamental studies and applications. However, conventional MS membranes are hampered by the problems of uncontrolled molecular interactions, low separation efficiency, and energy and time-consuming fabrications. Here, we show an effective plasma engineering of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dot (NGQD)-inorganic nanocomposites composed with NGQDs and alumina-based hollow fibers (AHFs) with controlled properties and tunable local physicochemical environments for size-selective molecular separations. Detailed spectroscopic study and membrane characterization suggest that the plasma-synthesized NGQDs with defined zero-dimensional (0D) carbon-based sp2 hybridization, tunable nitrogen dopants and surface functional groups can affect the properties of the GQD-inorganic nanocomposites, therefore controlling molecular separation between the nanocomposites and molecules with different dye molecular weights (MWs). Moreover, the NGQDs can further induce the formation of nanochannel and control the nanopore structures of the nanocomposites, leading a significant molecular separation performance with a high 92.68 separation factor (S) for the mixed molecules with a 200 Da MW difference and a high water permeability up to 36.64 L m−2 h−1 bar−1. Our work provides a step for the engineering of functional nanocomposites for fundamental studies of molecular transport and emerging applications including catalysis, green energy, optoelectronics, biomedical and environmental applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Molecular separation (MS)-enabled technologies with fast and simple operation, high separation efficiency, and low energy input are highly desired and important for fundamental studies and wide-range applications including clean water, energy generation and storage, electronics and semiconductor manufacturing, pharmaceuticals and fine chemicals production, biomedical and environmental applications [1–5]. Despite several technologies including distillation, pressure- and/or temperature-swing adsorption, and extraction are developed for MS, those methods are usually hampered with the problems of low separation efficiency, energy and time-consuming procedures, and complicated equipment [1,6–8]. Nanofiltration (NF) membrane-based approach is a promising method for MS with the advantages of high water permeability and separation efficiency, easy and scalable operation, and high stability in harsh conditions [9–12]. Moreover, precisely controlling the local physicochemical environments such as effects of adsorption, size-sieving, and electrostatic interaction and pore nanostructures of the membranes can further improve molecular separation. However, it is still lacking effective engineering to fabricate membranes with defined structures and tunable properties to maximize the MS separation performances.

Toward this goal, we propose a nanotechnology-enabled fabrication of water aqueous-derived graphene quantum dot (GQD) polyamide polymer via interfacial polymerization in the alumina-base substrate as GQD-inorganic nanocomposites as the MS membranes using the combination of plasma engineering and interfacial polymerization (IP). Recent studies demonstrate that nanomaterials-based membranes process tremendous potential for the development of MS membranes [13–16]. The incorporation of nanomaterials including nanoparticles (NPs), carbon nanotubes (CNTs), nanowires, and nanosheets such as graphene oxides and MXene nanosheets into the selective layer can further enhance the separation performances of the MS membranes [13–16]. The GQD represents a unique zero-dimensional (0D) carbon sp2 hybridization nanostructures with quantum transition-regulated band structures, exceptional thermal and chemical stabilities, tunable surface functionalities, and good biocompatibility are attractive materials for clean energy, optoelectronics, nanocatalysis, biomedical applications and membrane-based separations with high separation efficiencies and water transport [17–22]. However, current production methods of GQDs, including top-down and bottom-up methods, are normally involved with a long preparation time, complicated steps, toxic chemicals and elevated temperatures, making it difficult for large-scale production [23–26]. Plasma-based technologies, particularly atmospheric-pressure microplasmas, has been shown as an efficient and green method for producing nanomaterials including metal nanoparticles, semiconductors quantum dots, and graphene-based nanomaterials, due to the high chemical reactivities provided by the reactive ions and radicals generated in the plasmas.

Polyamide (PA)-based IP is a simple and structure-controllable method for membrane preparation [27–30], compared with other methods including layer-by-layer self-assembly (LBL) [31–33], surface modification [34,35], surface grafting [36–38]. In addition, the PA-based membranes show good mechanical and chemical stabilities and high water permeability [39,40]. On the other hand, alumina-based hollow fiber (AHF) membranes display the advantages on the large-packed volumes and broad active surfaces per unit volume of hollow fibers, making them attractive materials for molecular separation applications. Moreover, AHF membranes with hybrid and asymmetrical structures by employing inorganic layers as substrates and polymeric layers as selective layers can enhance the physical and chemical properties of the membranes, leading outstanding performance for molecular separation [41,42].

Here we report the plasma engineering of GQD-inorganic nanocomposites with tunable molecular interactions as the MS membranes with superior molecular separation performance, high water transport, and long-term stabilities (Scheme 1 (a)). The nitrogen-doped GQDs (NGQDs) with monodispersed size distribution and controllable surface functionalization are synthesized from membrane precursors using customized microplasma process in aqueous solutions at ambient conditions without toxic and strong chemicals, high temperature and vacuum conditions (Scheme 1(b), Fig. S2 and Fig. S3). The GQD-inorganic membranes are fabricated using a customized IP process with the plasma-synthesized GQD and AHF prepared by solvent-free phase separation (NIPS) method (Scheme 1(c)) [43–45]. The detail spectroscopic and membrane studies suggest that the plasma-engineered GQDs with tunable surface functional groups and nitrogen dopants can not only enhance the interactions with molecules but also improve the surface polarities and nanoporous structures of the membranes. Moreover, the asymmetrical structures of GQD-inorganic nanocomposites provide the synergy of mechanical and chemical stabilities supported by the AHF support layers and enhanced selectivity of molecular separation provided by the GQD-based polymer seelctive layers. The plasma-engineered GQD-inorganic membranes show the outstanding molecular separation properties including a significant separation factor (S) value of 92.68 for mixed molecules with a 200 Da MW difference, a high water permeability up to 36.64 L m−2 h−1 bar−1, and an excellent dye rejection up to 99.31 %. It is worth noting that the unique AHF-based substrates allow the large-scaled membrane modules, showing the potential for scalable separation processes in the industrial setting. Our work opens an avenue for the engineering of functional nanocomposite for fundamental studies of molecular transport in composite materials and emerging applications including clean water, green energy, pharmaceuticals production, food and agricultural safety, and environmental protection.

The GQD-inorganic membranes were fabricated using a customized IP process with the plasma-synthesized GQD and AHF prepared by NIPS method. Details can be found in the Supporting information (Figs. S3–S6). Briefly, Figs. S2 and S3 show the schematic depiction of the spinning and sintering processes of AHF preparation, respectively. The organic polyamide selective layer was created on the shell side of the alumina hollow fiber substrate via interfacial polymerization, as shown in Fig. S7. The GQDs were synthesized using a microplasma process with argon (Ar) gas at ambient conditions (Fig. S8). [28,46–48] The plasma conditions were kept at a 9.0 mA current,0.7 % wt PEI solution 20 ml and a variety of reaction times (5, 10, 15, and 20 min). The PA layer with a 0.7 wt% PEI concentration was used for the GQD-inorganic nanocomposites because it offered the optimized combination of membrane separation performance including high water permeability and molecular rejection. Details can be found in the Supporting Information (Figs. S9–S15).

Polyamide-based membranes were kept in DI water for further study and characterization. Before characterization, the alumina hollow fiber, TFC, and TFN membrane were stored in a vacuum environment for at least 24 h. SEM was used to examine the surface morphology and thickness of alumina hollow fiber membranes as well as the as-prepared membranes. The samples were sputtered coated with a thin layer of platinum prior to characterization to boost the electric conductivities without affecting the surface shape. CFP was used to determine the pore size distribution of alumina HF. Prior to the test, the HF was soaked in Galwick, a typical wetting liquid with a surface tension of 15.9 dyn cm−1. A contact angle method with 3 μL per drop was used to test the membrane hydrophilicity. XPS was used to examine the chemical composition and chemical bonding on the surface of HF, and TFN membranes.

Permeability and nanofiltration tests were performed at room temperature using a home-made “outside-in” and cross-flow filtration system with an effective area of approximately 2.4 cm2 (Fig. S1) [45,49]. Before collecting the following data, the alumina hollow fiber substrate and prepared membranes were pre-pressed under 3.5 bar with feed solutions for at least 30 min to achieve a steady state, then reduced the pressure back to 3 bar for another 30 min to stabilize the membrane performance while leaving the permeate side open to atmospheric conditions. The succeeding permeate was collected at 5-min intervals, and at least three data intervals were chosen to obtain the mean permeance values while preserving the transmembrane pressure at 2 bar. Eq. (1) calculated the pure water permeance (PWP) (1) [50].

MWCO of PA membranes can be done with neutral organic solutes, such as PEG molecules of varying molecular weight or monosaccharides. The MWCO was calculated from the rejection of PEG-200, PEG-400, PEG-600, and PEG-1000 in this study. In order to prevent concentration polarization, fouling, and osmotic pressure from occurring if the concentration of PEG in the feed solution was too high, a concentration of 500 ppm was chosen for all PEG molecules. The concentrations of organic solutes in the feed and permeate solutions were measured using a COD analyzer. And its equivalent rejection (R) was calculated using Eq. (2) [51]

The pore size distribution was calculated by probability density function that was established based on the following consumption: (1) The steric or hydrodynamic interaction between the neutral organic solutes and membrane pores can be ignored; (2) The mean pore size of the as-prepare membrane equals the Stokes radius of the organic solute with a rejection of 50 % (which can be estimated in the curve of MWCO); (3) The distribution of the membrane pore size is characterized by geometric standard deviation of the PDF curve, which is the ratio between the Stokes radius with a rejection 84.13 % to that with a rejection of 50 % [52]. Most commonly used dye molecules were selected including Methylene Blue (MEB), Crystal Violet (CV), Victoria Blue B (VB), and Congo Red (CR) for evaluating the PA TFC and TFN membranes’ dye rejection test. Solutions with single dye molecule were used for the rejection tests, and the concentration of dye solutions were maintained at 20 ppm. The dye concentration of feed and permeate solution were detected by UV–vis spectrophotometer. Fig. S9 shows the UV–vis spectrum of feed solution used in this work. The rejection of dye was determined by Eq. (2) .

Similar to dye/salt and salt/salt separation system, the separation factor (S) is introduced to evaluate the separation performance in the dye/dye separation system, and S was calculated by Eq. (5) [55]

The mixed dye separation performed in this work was carried out by using a mixed dye solution as feed solution during the NF experiment. Dye molecules were mixing with 1:1 volumetric ratio of two different single dye solution. The as-prepared mixed solution was continuously stirring prior the NF testing, and there was no aggregation observed in the mixed solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of GQD-inorganic membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The GQD-inorganic membranes were prepared using a customized stepwise procedure including the plasma synthesis of GQDs, IP process of the PA-based selective layers, and NIPS fabrication of the AHF supported layers. The PA selective layers were prepared and optimized by adjusting the concentration of aqueous monomers. Note that the PA layer with a 0.7 wt% PEI concentration was used for the GQD-inorganic nanocomposites because it offered the optimized combination of membrane separation performance including high water permeability and molecular rejection. The PA membranes with a concentration of 0.7 % PEI is abbreviated as the pristine PA membrane in this work. Details of membrane synthesis are provided in the Supporting Information section S2-S6 and Figs. S2–S15.

UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy was used to study the absorption of various electronic states of plasma-synthesized GQDs derived from PEI. Fig. 1 (a) shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of plasma GQDs synthesized under a 9.0 mA current with different reaction time (5, 10,15 and 20 min). The displayed absorption bands indicate the existence of various electronic states in the synthesized GQDs form the branched polyethylenimine (PEI) solution. The highest absorption at 281 nm for each spectrum can be attributed to the π→π* transition of the CC bond and possibly the n→π* transition of the CN bond in the graphene-like structures [23,56,57]. The results suggested that the microplasma could initiate the synthesis of GQDs. In addition, the absorption peak was found to be increased as the increased plasma treatment time, indicating more GQDs generated with increased plasma treatment time. The quantum transitions of plasma GQDs were further studied by the photoluminescent (PL) spectroscopy. Fig. 1(b) shows a contour plot of the PL intensity obtained as an excitation and emission function. The result shows the maximum PL intensity (shown in red) with the excitation and emission at 325 and 400 nm, respectively, representing the radiation recombination centers. A wide range of radiative recombination centers can be attributed to a variety of energy levels induced by surface functional groups, defects, dopants and edge states. Moreover, the emissions of GQDs were red-shifted as the increased plasma synthesis time, suggesting more functional groups attached on GQD surfaces with increased plasma synthesis time. Those surface functional groups, defects, dopants and edge states could be useful active site to interact with water and/or molecules to enhance the molecular separation. Importantly, the functional groups on the GQDs can not only help the hybridization of the GQD-inorganic membranes by the functional group-induced chemical environments but also enhance the membrane properties including tunable surface polarities, chemical interaction with molecules, and nanochannel structure formation, leading the high water permeability and molecular size-selective separations.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was performed to evaluate the surface morphologies of the prepared membranes. Fig. 2 shows the SEM images of prepared membranes. It is noted that pure and GQD membranes were effectively produced on the shell of AHF using the developed IP process. The pristine PA membrane exhibits a rigid-and-valley structure because of the uncontrollable diffusion of the aqueous monomer [21,39,58] during the IP reaction. This result could be due to the vigorous polymerization reaction at the interface [19,39], and the asymmetric dissipation of heat caused the undesirable protrusions on the membrane surface [59]. In contrast, the GQD membranes show the flatten-plane morphologies with the nodular-like structures filled with spherical globules, as seen in Fig. 2(a). Initial flattening might be attributed to the steric hindrance of GQDs, which could limit the diffusion of PEI via aqueous solution to the interface during the IP process. Through the incorporation of GQDs into the aqueous solution, membranes with a lower crosslinking degree and a smoother surface (lacking evident extrusions) were produced. With an increase in plasma treatment duration, the product yield of GQDs rose, and their surface morphology changed from a flatten-plane to a granular structure. Fig. 2(b) and Fig. S15 indicates that the thicknesses of the pristine and GQD membranes. It is found that the thicknesses of the selective layers rose progressively (from 36.6 to 154.8 nm) as plasma-treatment duration increased (from 0 to 20 min). According to Freger's kinetic model, the diffusivity is proportional to the thickness of the PA selective layer. The greater the monomer diffusivity during IP process, the thicker the selective layer can develop [60]. On the basis of the SEM characterization, it is suggested that GQDs can not only modify the surface morphologies of the membranes but also probably induce the nanoporous structures within the membranes [61,62], providing helpful morphologies and channels for water transport and molecular separations.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was utilized to evaluate the chemical composition and cross-linking degree of pure PA and GQD membranes. Fig. 3 shows the XPS spectra of the samples. Three major peaks including C1s, N1s, and O1s with the binding energies (BEs) of 285.0, 399.5, and 531.0 eV, respectively, were detected for PA membranes [50,63] (Fig. 3(a)). On the other hand, peaks comprise of Al 2s and Al 2p, with BEs 118.0, and 74.0 eV [64], respectively, were measured for AHF membranes (Fig. S10). As the PA films were deposited on the surfaces of AHF membranes, the peaks of Al2s and Al2p were vanished, suggesting that PA networks successfully coated on the AHF substrates during the fabrication (Fig. 3(a)). The C1s, O1s, and N1s XPS spectra of PA and GQD membranes were further deconvoluted to study the chemical bonds, and the data is shown in Fig. 3(b), (c) and S16. The analyzed C1s spectra show three chemical bonds of C–C (285.0 eV), C–N (285.8 eV), and N–CO (287.8 eV) (Fig. 3(b)) [65,66] while the O1s spectra show chemical bonds of N–CO (531 eV), –OH (532.2 eV) and C–CO (533.3 eV) [67]. The new –OH bond at 532.2 eV was attributed from the hydroxyl group in GQDs, indicating that the GQDs were integrated with the PA networks. The ratios of hydroxyl group in the fabricated samples rose from 12.43 to 17.44 % as plasma synthesis time increased, suggesting more GQDs were synthesized and incorporated with the membranes. The result is in agreed with the UV–vis absorbance and SEM studies. The peaks of N–CO in C1s, O1s, and N1s are possibly due to the polymerization of amino groups (-NH2) in the aqueous solution and acyl groups (Cl–CO) in the organic solution [68,69]. In the selective layer, the peak of O–CO was ascribed to the hydrolysis of unreacted acyl groups in TMC. The absence of a substantial shift in the peaks of the C1s, O1s, and N1s in the XPS spectra suggests that the primary polymerization between PEI and TMC was occurred. The observation is supported by the fact that the diffusion rate of GQDs was slower than that of amine monomers. The ratios of C–CO, N–CO, and –OH bonds are reported in Table S1. The fractions of N–CO bonds in the prepared samples were decreased as the plasma reaction time was increased. This result suggests the reduced crosslinking degree due to the insertion of GQDs, hence facilitating the generation of a loose structure. Overall, the XPS study indicates not only that the GQD-inorganic membranes with tunable chemical environments were prepared but also that the plasma-functionalized GQDs were successfully integrated into the PA networks in the composite membranes. The fabricated GQD-inorganic membranes with different chemical environments should be useful to study the factor to control the water transport and molecular separations in nanocomposites.

The surface hydrophilicity of pure PA and GQD membranes were additionally analyzed by assessing the static contact angles. As shown in Fig. S17, as the plasma synthesis time was increased from 0 to 20 min, the contact angle fell from 90 to 64°, indicating that the membranes became more hydrophilic after GQDs were incorporated into the polyamide system. The insertion of GQDs in the membranes can enhance the surface hydrophilicity by the improved hydrogen bonds between the water and abundant oxygen-containing group in GQDs [20,70]. Moreover, the increased surface areas created by the granular structures and the nanochannels induced from the GQDs can further enhance the water adsorption capacities of the GQD membranes [62,67,71].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation performance study of GQD membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The separation performance of prepared GQD-inorganic membranes was studied by measuring the water permeability and rejection rates of cango red (CR) in Fig. 4 (a) and Fig. S18. Fig. 4(a) shows the effect of GQD loading mass in the membranes on the water permeability and rejection rate of CR. It is noted that the water permeability was increased with the increased GQD amounts in the membranes. Interestingly, the GQD membranes not only show a larger water permeability than the PA membranes but also without losing the rejection rate of CR. The rejection rate remains greater than 99.9 % for all the plasma prepared samples. Moreover, the flow was found to be increased by more than 300 % (from 8.95 to 40.64 L m−2 h−1 bar−1) for the GQD membrane with the greatest GQD concentration. It can be explained that GQDs promote the generation of thicker, looser, and more hydrophilic selective layers with more nanochannel in the membranes, leading an improvement for the membrane performance with high rejection rate of CR and high water permeability. While larger selective layer could reduce the water transportation, the looser structure could significantly decrease the permeation resistance and therefore enhance the water transport (Fig. S19) [20,21].

The molecular sieving capabilities of prepared GQD-inorganic membranes were further studied by measuring the rejection rates of dyes with different MWs and charges, including methylene blue (MEB), crystal violet (CV), victoria blue B (VB), and CR. Fig. 4(b) shows the rejection rates of different dyes by pristine PA and GQD membranes fabricated with different plasma reaction times. Noted that the rejection rates of CV and MEB were reduced monotonically with as increased plasma reaction time, suggesting that the GQD concentration in the membranes could control the rejection rates of dyes with different MWs. Take MEB rejection as an example; the rejection rate for the pristine PA membrane maintained a high level of performance at 95 %, however, after 20 mints plasma reaction time, the rejection rate dropped substantially to 7.32 %. As the degree of crosslinking decreases, the selective layer becomes increasingly lax, thereby facilitating the penetration of small molecules through the polyamide networks [22,62]. On the basis of Eq. (5) (see Experimental section), we further compared the rejection rate of MEB to those of other dyes and estimated the selectivity using the separation factor (S) (Fig. S20). It is noticed that the all GQD-inorganic membranes show higher S values for delta MWs of 200 and 400 Da than those of PA membranes. In particular, the GQD-inorganic membranes with15 mints plasma synthesis time exhibits the significant S value of 92.68 for the mixed molecules with a 200 Da MW in our work. The above result suggests that the plasma treatment effectively decreases the crosslinking degree of the membranes, resulting in fabricated GQD membranes with larger and controlled nanopore structures. In addition, the plasma time may precisely regulate the nanopores of the fabricated GQD membranes to accomplish effective separation when the delta MW is greater than 100 Da.

The Polyethylene glycol (PEG) molecules with different MWs including 200, 400, 600, and 1000 g mol−1 were used to study the molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) values of the prepared GQD membranes. As shown in Fig. 4(c), the MWCO was increased steadily from 350 to 580 Da for PA and GQD membranes. Increasing MWCO for GQD membranes suggest that the GQD membranes can separate smaller molecules, showing the potential for molecular separation. It can be explained that the integration of GQDs expanded the pores of polyamide networks, making it possible to separate small molecules [72,73]. The pore size distribution of the GQD membranes was calculated using the possibility density function, and the result is shown in Fig. 4(d). Table S2 also provides the summary of the matching mean pore size and standard deviation of the pore size distribution curve. The PA membranes show a lower average pore size of 0.659 nm while the GQD membranes exhibit larger pore sizes ranging from 0.706 to 0.823 nm with the increased GQD concentrations. The results suggest that the insertion of GQDs could loosen polyamide networks by expanding the membrane pores [72,74]. In addition, the PEG molecule remains unchanged as a charge substitute during the MWCO test. The incorporation of a negatively-charged GQD nanofiller membrane (Fig. S21) affects separation performance via the electrostatic repulsion effect [75–77]. A comparison of Fig. 4(b) and (c) rejection results reveals a decrease in the rejection of dyes with the same negative charge as the nanocomposite. In contrast, compounds with positive charge properties are increasingly rejected by the membrane. The membrane's efficacy is substantially influenced by its electrical properties. We further use the UV–vis results to determine the potential adsorption behavior [78](Fig. S22). In experiments involving negatively charged dyes, the UV–vis spectrum absorption peaks indicate that there is no change in adsorption behavior. Conversely, when testing positively charged dyes, the UV spectrum declines slightly and reaches adsorption saturation within 10 min, indicating that the results pertaining to the separation test of conservation and adsorption. Based on the above test, plasma procedure induces a major mechanism for pore size and charge ability, which is synergistically advantageous for precise molecule separation.

Chemical stability of the GQD membranes was further studied under extremely acidic and alkaline conditions with the pH values of 1 and 14, respectively. Before performing the separation test, GQD membranes were initially immersed in an acidic or alkaline solutions for 30 mints and then in pH neutral DI-water for another 30 mints. The stability was assessed by repeating the above process four times. As shown in Fig. 4(e), acidic solutions have little effect on either the rejection or water permeability, revealing that the GQD membranes were stable under acidic conditions. In contrast, the separation performance was deteriorated under the basic condition, as demonstrated in Fig. 4(f). The degradation may be attributable to the hydrolysis of amide groups, which reduces the degree of crosslinking in PA networks [1,44,67]. The result indicates that the GQD membranes can withstand acidic wastewater. The long-term stability test of the GQD membrane was carried out by prolonging the filtration test up to 72 h. CR was used as the feed solution for the long-term test at a transmembrane pressure of 2 bar, and the result is shown in Fig. 4(g). In the first 2 h, the permeance decreased slightly due to the dye cake formation by the deposition of dye molecules on the membrane surface or pores. The permeance then stabilized around 36.64 L m−2 h−1 bar−1and no discernible breakdown of CR during their rejection. Hence, the plasma-fabricated GQD membrane has the potential for use in environmental MS applications due to its unique QD properties.

Mixed solution of dyes with different molecular weights was used to test the molecular separation ability of GQD membranes using UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, as shown schematically in Fig. 5 (a) and Fig. S9. CR and MEB are selected for the test because no reactions or interruptions are occurred between CR and MEB. Moreover, the CR and MEB can show distinct absorption peaks in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum, making it possible to analysis the molecular separation performance precisely. Fig. 5(b) shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of feed mixed solution of CB and MEB and permeated solutions using GQD membranes with different plasma process time. First, two major absorption peaks of CR and MEB located at 481 nm and 665 nm, respectively, were clearly observed in the mixed solution. Significantly, the CR absorption peaks are found to be flat under all circumstances, indicating that the CR molecules with high molecular weight are completely rejected with GQD membranes with different GQD concentrations. Moreover, the MEB absorption peaks in the permeated solution increased with the increased plasma time, showing a gradual decrease in the rejection of MEB molecules with low molecular weight using GQD membranes with varying GQD concentrations. Photographs of feed solution and filtration solution are shown in the inset of Fig. 5(b) for comparison. The solution containing both CR and MEB is a grayish-blue color, and no observable aggregation was observed. Importantly, the separation of mixed dyes yields the same result as the rejection of single dyes.

We further compare the separation performance of the plasma-fabricated GQD-inorganic membranes with other MS membranes. The result is summarized in Fig. 5(c) and Tables S3 and S4. Interestingly, the plasma-fabricated GQD membranes show the outstanding molecular separation properties with a high S value of 92.68 for mixed molecules with a 200 Da Mw difference, which is comparable with previous work (Fig. 5(c) and Table S4). Moreover, the plasma-fabricated GQD membranes also exhibit a high water permeability up to 36.64 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 and a high dye rejection up to 99.31 %, showing the advances to separate the molecules with large MWs (Tables S3 and S4).

Low-cost, high-efficiency and stable MS membranes are highly required for fundamental studies and applications including green energy, functional chemicals production, food and water safety, and environmental protection. In this work, we have developed a simple plasma engineering to fabricate GQD-inorganic nanocomposites used as the MS membranes with extremely high separation efficiency. The NGQDs with controllable N dopant configurations and surface functionalities can be synthesized using microplasmas under ambient conditions without toxic and strong chemicals, high temperature and vacuum conditions. The N dopant configurations and surface functionalities on the NGQDs can affect the local environment in the membranes and therefore enhance the membrane properties including tunable surface polarities, chemical interaction with molecules, and nanochannel structure formation, leading a high water permeability and molecular size-selective separations. The plasma-fabricated GQDs membranes show the outstanding membrane performance including a high S value (92.68) for mixed molecules with a 200 Da Mw difference, a high water permeability of 36.64 L m−2 h−1 bar−1, and a high dye rejection of 99.31 %. Our work provides a step for the new nanotechnology to fabricate functional nanocomposites for fundamental studies of molecular transport and emerging applications including clean water, green energy, pharmaceuticals production, food and agricultural safety, and environmental protection.
